title: Gluten-free chocolate chip cookies
subtitle: Chewy cookies with crisp edges made with almond and rice flour
excerpt: Chocolate chip cookies made entirely without wheat flour: almond flour provides the fat and chewiness while rice flour gives body and prevents them from being too dense. The result is a slightly flatter, chewier cookie than a standard recipe, with crisp edges and a soft centre that firms on cooling. Better for being dairy-free too, since the flavour of the chocolate comes through clearly without butter.
sourceNotes: Chocolate chip cookies are documented in American baking literature from the late 1930s, with the standard recipe attributed to Ruth Wakefield, Toll House Tried and True Recipes (1938, now in the public domain under US copyright duration rules). Nut-flour variations for those avoiding wheat are described in American and British domestic cookery and dietary guides from the mid-twentieth century, adapting the standard method to gluten-free flours.

recipe.freezeNotes: Freeze as baked and cooled cookies, or freeze balls of unbaked dough on a tray then transfer to a bag. Bake from frozen, adding 3 to 4 minutes.
recipe.batchNotes: Double the recipe. Bake in batches of 10 to 12 per tray.
recipe.makeAheadNotes: Refrigerating the dough for 30 minutes before baking firms it and reduces spreading. The baked cookies keep in an airtight tin for 5 days.

baking.bakeTemperatureNote: conventional oven. Fan at 160°C. Bake for 10 to 12 minutes until the edges are set and lightly golden. The centres will look underdone; they firm on cooling.

glossaryTerms.definition: Gluten-free cookie dough spread more than wheat dough during baking because there is no gluten network to hold the shape. Two techniques reduce spread: chilling the dough for at least 30 minutes before baking, which firms the fat so it melts more slowly in the oven; and spacing the cookies well apart on the tray, so the spread is free rather than merging. A slightly rounded ball of dough (rather than a flat disc) also helps. Cookies baked from very cold dough may need 1 to 2 extra minutes.

content.text: The main practical issue with gluten-free cookies is
. Chilling the dough first is the most effective fix. The cookies will still spread more than a wheat-flour version, but cold dough slows the spread enough to give a proper shape.

Beat the butter, caster sugar, and dark brown sugar together until fluffy. Beat in the egg and vanilla. Stir in the almond flour, rice flour, bicarbonate of soda, and salt until a soft dough forms. Fold in the chocolate chips. Chill the dough for 30 minutes.

Heat the oven to 180°C conventional (160°C fan). Line two baking trays with baking paper. Scoop tablespoon-sized balls of dough onto the trays, spacing them at least 5 cm apart. Bake for 10 to 12 minutes until the edges are set and lightly golden. The centres will look underdone; leave to cool on the tray for 5 minutes before transferring to a rack. They firm as they cool.
